name: Pelican Point
short_description: BLM rockhounding near Utah Lake with agate and jasper deposits. Free public access, perfect for beginners seeking colorful specimens in an accessible desert setting.
description:
Pelican Point sits on Bureau of Land Management property in Utah County, offering rockhounders easy access to agate and jasper formations. The site sits within the Basin and Range province where Quaternary alluvial deposits have exposed colorful silica-rich materials ideal for recreational collectors.

The terrain around Pelican Point features sandy washes and low-profile ridgelines typical of the Great Basin landscape. Rockhounders typically find nodular agates and banded jasper specimens in wash areas and along hillside exposures. The site is known for producing material in cream, tan, and rust tones common to central Utah's geologic character.

As a BLM location, Pelican Point welcomes casual collectors year-round without permits or fees. The landscape is relatively open, making it accessible for families and beginners. Parking is informal, with pullouts available near productive areas. Most productive hunting occurs in spring and fall when weather is moderate and visibility is optimal.

The combination of free access, beginner-friendly terrain, and reliable agate production makes Pelican Point a solid choice for day trips from Utah County communities. Bring plenty of water, sun protection, and a small backpack for specimens.
history: This area sits within the Basin and Range province where Quaternary alluvial and lacustrine deposits exposed silica-rich fluids and sediments that formed the agate and jasper nodules found here today. The location's proximity to ancient Lake Bonneville and subsequent pluvial activity concentrated chalcedony-based minerals in accessible wash gravels.
tips: Search wash bottoms and eroded hillsides where water has naturally exposed nodular agate and banded jasper specimens. Bring a small rock hammer and 10x loupe to assess translucency and banding patterns before collecting. Look for waxy-luster nodules in cream and tan tones - these typically indicate quality agate worth keeping. Focus on areas with recent erosion where specimens have weathered free from parent material.
terrain_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain at approximately 4,300 feet elevation. Sandy washes interspersed with low sagebrush and scattered rocks. Most productive areas are within 0.5 mile of parking with minimal elevation change. Footing is generally good on packed wash surfaces, though loose rock scattered on hillsides requires careful step placement.
best_season: October - April. Winter weather in this zone is mild, with occasional snow at higher elevations nearby. Spring and fall offer ideal temperatures and clearer visibility. Summer can exceed 90 degrees Fahrenheit with intense sun exposure and reduced comfort for extended hunting.
written_directions: From Provo, take US 89 south toward Utah Lake approximately 8 miles. Turn west toward the water and look for BLM access points and informal parking pullouts along the roads approaching Pelican Point. The site is accessible via local roads with signage for public land. GPS coordinates 40.267475, -111.84949 mark the productive area directly. High-clearance vehicle recommended for rough pullouts, though passenger cars can reach main access points.
road_conditions: Improved and unimproved roads lead to parking areas. Some pullouts may have washboard texture after winter weather. Passenger vehicles can reach main access points; high-clearance recommended for rough secondary pullouts.
parking_notes: Informal BLM pullouts and parking areas near productive zones. No developed parking lot. Capacity typically 2-4 vehicles per pullout. Arrive early on weekends to secure space.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Rockhounding allowed on open BLM land only; respect any posted closures.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Respect posted closure areas
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Provo, Utah - approximately 10 miles north with full services including gas, food, water, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers. Unpaved parking pullouts and sandy wash walking surfaces. No wheelchair access to productive areas. Sturdy footwear and sun protection essential. Limited shade available; bring hat and sunscreen.

faq.question: What agates and jasper can I find rockhounding at Pelican Point near Utah County?
faq.answer: Pelican Point is known for producing nodular agates and banded jasper in cream, tan, and rust tones. The agate specimens typically range from walnut to fist-sized with translucent to waxy luster. Banded chalcedony is also common. Most collectors find 3-8 quality specimens on a half-day outing.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free at Pelican Point Utah, or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Pelican Point is completely free with no permit required. The site is on Bureau of Land Management property where recreational collecting is allowed under FLPMA rules. You need only follow the 25 lb per day personal use limit and avoid motorized excavation.

faq.question: When is the best time to rockhound at Pelican Point for agate collecting?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Fall and spring offer comfortable temperatures (50-70 degrees Fahrenheit) and excellent visibility for spotting specimens. Summer heat exceeds 90 degrees with intense sun exposure, making mid-day hunting uncomfortable. Winter weather is typically mild at this elevation.

faq.question: Is Pelican Point rockhounding site beginner-friendly for families with kids?
faq.answer: Yes, Pelican Point is excellent for beginners and families with children ages 6 and up. Terrain is relatively flat with short walking distances from parking. No technical skills required - just walk wash bottoms and eroded hillsides to find agates. Bring water, sunscreen, and a small backpack for specimens.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need to rockhound effectively at Pelican Point?
faq.answer: Bring a small rock hammer or chisel for extracting specimens from matrix, a 10x hand loupe to assess translucency and banding, a backpack for carrying finds, sturdy hiking boots, sun hat, sunscreen, and 2-3 liters of water per person. A GPS unit or smartphone with offline maps is helpful for navigation.

faq.question: How far is Pelican Point rockhounding site from Provo, and what is the drive time?
faq.answer: Pelican Point is approximately 10 miles south of Provo via US 89 toward Utah Lake. Drive time is roughly 15-20 minutes depending on traffic. The site is easily accessible for day trips from Provo, Utah County, and nearby communities. Parking pullouts near the GPS coordinates mark productive collecting areas.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Provo. Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony at Pelican Point. No permit required, beginner-friendly, 25 lbs daily limit.
